supply_chain.dependencies: Principal raw material is recycled ferrous scrap; dependence on scrap metal availability and pricing; reliance on third parties for energy resources (electricity, natural gas, oil); zinc supply for galvanized steel products; graphite electrodes and ferroalloys
supply_chain.risks: Volatility and major fluctuations in prices and availability of scrap metal and scrap substitutes; potential inability to pass higher costs to customers; cost and availability of electricity, natural gas, oil subject to volatile market conditions; availability of adequate scrap supply for metals recycling operations; supply chain disruptions and labor shortages exacerbating equipment failures

regulatory_risks: Compliance with and changes in environmental and remediation requirements
Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA) - handling, recycling and disposal of solid and hazardous wastes
Comprehensive Environmental Response Compensation and Liability Act (CERCLA/Superfund) - liability for contaminated properties
Clean Water Act - discharge of wastewater, storm water, dredged or fill material
Clean Air Act - air permits and emission limits
Emergency Planning and Community Right-to-Know Act
Environmental regulations in Mexico for operations there
Increased environmental, greenhouse gas emissions and sustainability regulations from customers, investors or regulators
Potential new or more stringent environmental laws and regulations in the future
Governmental agencies may refuse to grant or renew licenses and permits required to operate
Foreign Corrupt Practices Act and other anti-bribery laws for international operations
Office of Foreign Assets Control regulations
Potential changes in tax laws including sunset of certain tax laws
Trade policies or restrictions including tariffs or quotas

sanction_risks: Global steelmaking overcapacity and imports of steel into the United States from foreign producers including China and Vietnam
Dumping and subsidy abuses by foreign steel producers
Some foreign steel producers owned, controlled or subsidized by foreign governments
Tariffs, duties or quotas may expire, be relaxed, repealed or circumvented by importers
Excessive imports of steel products exerting downward pressure on US steel prices
Foreign currency exchange rate fluctuations affecting imports
